coredata.dc:title: Development of sensitive biomass xylan-based carbon dots fluorescence sensor for quantification detection Cu2+ in real water and soil
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Zhang, Yingying

Cheng, Weixia
Huang, Chaobo
coredata.dc:description: Copper ions (Cu2+) pose significant risks to both human health and the environment as they tend to accumulate in soil and water. To address this issue, an innovative method using biomass-derived fluorescent carbon dots (D-CDs) synthesized via a hydrothermal process, with xylan serving as the carbon source was developed. D-CDs solution exhibited remarkable sensitivity and selectivity as a fluorescence sensor for Cu2+, boasting a low detection threshold of 0.64 μM. In order to facilitate real-time monitoring of Cu2+, solid-state fluorescent nanofiber membrane (NFD-CDs) through electrospinning was engineered. Additionally, D-CDs demonstrated successful Cu2+ detection in various real water samples, including those sourced from Xuanwu Lake, the Yangtze River, tap water, and bottled water, with accurate recovery rates observed. As a result, this research introduces a dual-mode analytical system for onsite detection of Cu2+ in real scenarios. By harnessing biomass-derived fluorescent CDs materials and solid-state fluorescence sensors, this approach offers a promising solution for addressing the challenges associated with Cu2+ contamination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Cu2+
Real-time detection
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescence nanofibers membrane NFD-CDs
Detection Cu 2+ in real soil samples
Detection Cu 2+ in real water samples
Construction of NFD-CDs for detection Cu 2+
Biocompatibility application of D-CDs and NFD-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and schematic diagram of xylan derived biomass CDs based nanofiber membranes serves as solid state fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ detection.

(A) Schematic diagram of D-CDs synthesis process. (B) Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra of D-CDs. (C) TEM image of P-CDs. (D) Size distribution of D-CDs. (E) FTIR spectrum of D-CDs. (F) High-resolution spectrum of C1s of D-CDs. (G) High-resolution spectrum of S2p.

(A) Excitation characteristic of D-CDs. (B) The selectivity and anti-interference ability of D-CDs and D-CDs + Cu2+ with various substances including (20 μM, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ni+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, F−, CN−, ACO−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, SCN−, HS− and Hcy) (λ ex = 310 nm, silt = 4 nm), respectively. (C) Binding mechanism of D-CDs with Cu2+. (D) Fluorescence spectra of D-CDs with various amounts of Cu2+ (0–150 μM). Inset: Scatter plot of changes in fluorescence intensity of D-CDs with increasing concentration of Cu2+. (E) Linear relationship of D-CDs with Cu2+ concentrations. (F) Time stability and fluorescence stability of D-CDs and D + Cu2+ (λ ex = 310 nm, silt = 4 nm).

(A) Preparation of solid-state fluorescent nanofiber membrane NFD-CDs and used for detection of Cu2+. (B) Color change of NFD-CDs under Uv-vis light (365 nm). (C) SEM images of NFD-CDs (scale bar: 10 um). (D) EDS analysis of NFD-CDs. (E) Size distribution of NFD-CDs. (F) Color change of NFD-CDs + Cu2+ under Uv-vis light (365 nm). (G) SEM images of NFD-CDs + Cu2+ (scale bar: 20 um). (H) EDS analysis of NFD-CDs + Cu2+. (I) Size distribution of NFD-CDs + Cu2+.

(A) Fluorescence color change of soil extract solution mixed with D-CDs for Cu2+ detection. (B) Fluorescence intensity change of soil extract solution mixed with D-CDs for Cu2+ detection. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Biocompatibility of D-CDs with Chinese cabbage seeds. (B) Biocompatibility of NFD-CDs with adult zebrafish. (C) Cabbage survival rate of D-CDs. (D) Zebrafish survival rate of NFD-CDs.
Comparison of the detection performance of currently fluorescence sensor.
Detection of Cu2+ in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Development of sensitive biomass xylan-based carbon dots fluorescence sensor for quantification detection Cu2+ in real water and soil

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors (Xiuyuan Feng and Yingying Zhang) are contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper ions (Cu2+) pose significant risks to both human health and the environment as they tend to accumulate in soil and water. To address this issue, an innovative method using biomass-derived fluorescent carbon dots (D-CDs) synthesized via a hydrothermal process, with xylan serving as the carbon source was developed. D-CDs solution exhibited remarkable sensitivity and selectivity as a fluorescence sensor for Cu2+, boasting a low detection threshold of 0.64 μM. In order to facilitate real-time monitoring of Cu2+, solid-state fluorescent nanofiber membrane (NFD-CDs) through electrospinning was engineered. Additionally, D-CDs demonstrated successful Cu2+ detection in various real water samples, including those sourced from Xuanwu Lake, the Yangtze River, tap water, and bottled water, with accurate recovery rates observed. As a result, this research introduces a dual-mode analytical system for onsite detection of Cu2+ in real scenarios. By harnessing biomass-derived fluorescent CDs materials and solid-state fluorescence sensors, this approach offers a promising solution for addressing the challenges associated with Cu2+ contamination.

ce:section.ce:para: With the swift pace of economic, industrial, and agricultural growth, environmental pollution has emerged as a paramount global challenge. Among these concerns, the contamination by heavy metal ions has garnered significant attention from both the public and the scientific community. Notably, the pollution stemming from copper ions (Cu2+) in water sources has raised alarm due to its resistance to biodegradation, enduring presence in the environment, and propensity for bioaccumulation in organisms. This has led to severe and irreversible repercussions on ecosystem integrity and human well-being [1–4]. Recognizing the gravity of the situation, esteemed bodies such as the World Health Organization (WHO) and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have established safe intake levels for Cu2+ in drinking water at 2.0 mg/L (∼30 μM) and 1.3 mg/L (∼20 μM) respectively. In national strategies like the ‘Twelfth Five-Year Plan for the Comprehensive Prevention and Control of Heavy Metal Pollution (China),’ Cu2+ is categorized as a significant metal pollutant necessitating stringent management. Hence, the precise detection and real-time monitoring of Cu2+ concentrations in aquatic environments assume critical importance in ensuring human health and environmental safety.

Currently, the primary analytical methods employed for detecting Cu2+ in aquatic environments encompass inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and various wet chemical procedures [4–8]. These methodologies are favored for their exceptional selectivity, sensitivity, and precision [9–11]. Nonetheless, they do pose certain constraints, such as steep costs, intricate operation procedures, reliance on skilled personnel, substantial sample consumption, labor-intensive sample preparation, prolonged testing durations, and the necessity for chemical derivatization [12–16]. Furthermore, these techniques demand substantial infrastructure support, and their intricate setup and operation impede mobility, rendering them unsuitable for swift field testing and monitoring requirements. Hence, there's an urgent need for the development of cost-effective, user-friendly, portable, and highly discerning detection instruments for Cu2+ detection.

Compared with the above traditional detection methods, the fluorescence analysis method has the advantages of high sensitivity, strong selectivity and simple operation [17–23]. As a new type of fluorescent probe, carbon dot (CDs) has the characteristics of good water solubility, environmentally friendly, low toxicity and resistance to photobleaching [24,25]. In recent years, fluorescent CDs prepared from biomass materials (i.e., plants, vegetables, agricultural and forestry waste) have been widely used to detect and analyze substances owning their non-toxic, harmless, green and renewable advantages [26,27]. With the abundant and renewable natural products in nature as the initial carbon source, it not only meets the needs of large-scale synthesis of biomass fluorescence CDs, but also promotes the development of sustainable applications [1,28,29]. It is worth mentioning that biomass fluorescence CDs show strong sensing ability for analytes because of their excellent fluorescence properties and rich functional groups on the surface [30,31]. Fluorescence quenching or fluorescence enhancement caused by target molecules can be used to detect trace elements, biological small molecules, biological macromolecules and other substances in environment [32–34]. Zhang and co-workers used carp scales as the initial carbon source to prepare nitrogen-rich CDs with low cytotoxicity, photobleaching resistance and good fluorescence stability through a simple and economical hydrothermal reaction [35]. With a quantum yield of 6.9 %, it can be used as a safe and reliable fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of Fe3+ content in human serum with high recovery and low standard deviation. Song et al. [24], designed a CDs from lignin with high fluorescence performance for tumor-targeted labeling. Xu et al. [17], used cellulose as the initial carbon source for conduction CDs as fluorescent material for extreme pH sensing. Brink et al. [36], developed a water-soluble fluorescent CDs from pine-cone for detection of Cu2+ with a low detection limit of 0.005 μg/mL. Therefore, the synthesis of fluorescent CDs using biomass material as carbon source is of great significance for the detection of target analytes.

Xylan, a prevalent lignocellulosic biomass, primarily consists of carbon (C), hydrogen (H), and oxygen (O), with carbon making up over 40 % of its composition [37,38]. This high carbon content is crucial for forming the carbon core structure in carbon dots (CDs) during their nucleation phase. Consequently, xylan serves as an effective, sustainable carbon source for creating functional fluorescent CDs, offering advantages in detecting Cu2+. However, to address the challenges of storing and handling the liquid form of fluorescent CDs, which include risks of leakage and difficulties in on-site detection, there is a pressing need to develop a carrier system. This system would integrate with nanofiber membrane with CDs solution, facilitating in situ and real-time detection of Cu2+ with high sensitivity.

Herein, in order to achieve high sensitivity and selective detection of Cu2+, biomass fluorescence CDs (D-CDs) was designed and synthesized using xylan as carbon source. D-CDs could achieve quantitative and qualitative analysis of Cu2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity by using fluorescence emission spectrum and colorimetric analysis with the detection limit of 0.64 μM. For detection Cu2+ in real time and in suit, D-CDs was further composited with nanofiber membrane (PCL/PLA) to construct a fluorescent solid platform (NFD-CDs) (Fig. 1 ) by electrospinning technology. NFD-CDs also realized detection of exogenous Cu2+ with accurate colorimetric detection capability. This work provides a new method for the visual detection of Cu2+ by using biomass materials.

Xylan (from birch, 95 %), 2,2’-Dithiodibenzoic acid (DTSA, 98 %), EtOH Polycaprolactone (PCL, Mn = 45,000), polylactic acid (PLA, Mw = 60,000), hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP, 99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Zebrafish was purchased from Shanghai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Real soil samples were obtained from bamboo forests, small rivers, and ponds from campus, respectively.) Cabbage seeds were purchased from the local wet market.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEM 1400 and JEM-2100 UHR transmission electron microscope. After the 10 ppm CDs solution was homogenized by ultrasound, it was dropped on the surface of the copper mesh. After the solvent volatilized at room temperature, it was placed on the sample table of the microscope for TEM and HRTEM morphology observation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Thermo electron Nicolet iZ10) was used to characterize the chemical composition and functional groups of samples in the region of 500–4000 cm. Cold-field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Regulus 8100, Japan Hitachi Co., Ltd.) was used to characterize the morphology. Following the drying process, the fluorescent nanofiber membrane was cut to a certain size and affixed to the surface of the sample table, and gold particles were sprayed to enhance the electrical conductivity of the sample, and then the sample table was put into a microscope for morphological observation. The prepared sample table was then placed under a microscope for morphological examination. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (AXIS UltraDLD, Shimazu, UK) was used to determine the composition. Fluorescence spectra was tested using a fluorescence spectrometer (FS5) (Edinburgh, Scotland, UK). The absorption spectra were determined by Read Max 1900 (Shanghai Flash Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Flash Flash).

According to the operation method of preparing CDs by hydrothermal synthesis provided in the reference [39], herein, xylan (0.1 g) was dissolved in 10.0 mL of ultra-pure water, followed by the gradual addition of 0.3 g of DTSA with continuous agitation to form a mixed solution. This solution was then transferred to a water bath set at 50.0 °C and stirred at 350 r/min for 1.0 h. Subsequently, the mixed solution was placed in a 50 mL autoclave, which was then subjected to a vacuum oven set at 0.1 MPa, 180.0 °C for 12.0 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and filtered using a PTFE syringe filter with a 0.22 μm aperture. The filtrate was then transferred to a cellulose dialysis bag with a molecular weight retention of 500.0 Da and dialyzed with deionized water for 48.0 h. Finally, the solution within the dialysis bag was collected and stored at 4 °C, namely D-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescence nanofibers membrane NFD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Following our established protocol, a mixture of PLA (12 wt%) and PCL (12 wt%) was dissolved in HFIP solution (10 mL) with overnight stirring [40,41]. The resulting electrospinning precursor solution was then loaded into a 5 mL syringe and spun into nanofibers under high voltage, with a feed rate of 1.3 mL/h using a syringe pump [42]. The distance between the needle tip and the rotating collector (300 r/min) was maintained at 18 cm, while a voltage of 18 kV was applied via a high voltage station. The resultant nanofiber membrane, termed PCL/PLA, was formed. Subsequently, the PCL/PLA nanofiber membrane underwent a physical impregnation process, wherein it was immersed in a D-CDs solution and vacuum-soaked for 2 h at room temperature. Afterward, the membrane was extracted from the solution and rinsed with ultra-pure water to remove any unbound fluorescent D-CDs. The resulting fluorescence-enhanced nanofiber membrane, NFD-CDs, was dried at 40 °C for further characterization.

Typically, 1 mL of D-CDs solution is injected into a quartz cuvette using a pipette gun. The concentration of D-CDs is diluted with deionized water to 2 mg/mL. Subsequently, different types of analytes (metal ions, non-metal examples, and biologically active substances: control, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, F−, Cl−, CN−, HPO4 2−, ClO−, NO2−, SCN−, HS− and Hcy) are added separately to the D-CDs solution to assess its selectivity. To evaluate the quantitative detection ability of D-CDs for Cu2+, spectroscopic experimental tests are performed by adding different concentrations of Cu2+ under the same assay conditions as described above. To assess the anti-interference ability of D-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of a mixture of 1 mL of deionized water and 1 mL of D-CDs (2 mg/mL) is measured in a 1 × 1 cm cuvette. This measurement is repeated after adding 10 μL of Cu2+ (0.01 mol/L) to the mixture. The immunity to interference is evaluated by comparing the fluorescence intensity values before and after the addition of Cu2+. All experiments are performed at room temperature and all measurements are repeated three times. The fluorescence intensity was recorded in the wavelength range of 350 nm–1000 nm with an excitation wavelength of 305 nm, silt: 4 nm.

In the naked eye test, NFD-CDs with the size of 2.0 cm (length) x 1.0 cm (width) was immersed in Cu2+ solution, and the fluorescence color change of NFD was observed under UV–vis lamp (365 nm) and sunlight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Cu 2+ in real soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure was as follows: soil samples were placed into centrifuge tubes and 40 mL of water was added, followed by ultrasonic extraction for 30 min. Subsequently, the samples were placed in a 75 °C water bath for 5 min, cooled to room temperature, and transferred to 50 mL volumetric bottles. The solution was then filtered through filter paper, centrifuged at 1000 r/min for 15 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the sample stock solution. Three parallel samples were prepared for each group, and the recovery rate was determined based on the fluorescence titration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Cu 2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water samples were taken from the Yangtze River. Xuanwu Lake Water, bottled water and tap water. First, the water sample was centrifuged at 10000 r/min for 15 min, and the upper liquid was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Under specific excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission spectra of Cu2+ detected by D-CDs in different real water samples were recorded, and the concentration of Cu2+ in real samples was calculated according to the fluorescence titration curve.

Biomass D-CDs was prepared by a simple hydrothermal synthesis method using xylan as the starting carbon source, as shown in Fig. 2A. The surface of D-CDs was rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which made D-CDs have excellent water solubility. Under sunlight, the solution of D-CDs was a yellow-brown. And under ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), the solution showed bright blue fluorescence, which proving that D-CDs has fluorescence properties, as shown in Fig. 2B inset. Then, ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra of D-CDs showed a maximum absorption wavelength at 310 nm, as shown in Fig. 2B. The morphology and particle size of D-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2C, D-CDs were uniformly dispersed spheroidal particles with particle size roughly distributed in the range of 1.5–3.5 nm, with an average particle size of about 2.3 nm (Fig. 2D). The chemical composition and structure of D-CDs were further characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). It can be seen from the FT-IR diagram in Fig. 2E that the absorption peak at 3450 cm−1 belongs to the OH stretching vibration. The wide absorption peak of 2940 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of the saturation = C-H-. The peak at 2650 cm−1 was attributed to SH of D-CDs. The sharp absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching vibration. The absorption peaks at 1511 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1 were the skeleton vibration of CC of D-CDs. The wide absorption peak of 1253–1260 cm−1 and the peak of 1134 cm−1 were the asymmetric and symmetrical stretching vibration of CO, respectively. The peaks of 820 cm−1 and 700 cm−1 both belonged to the flexural vibration of saturation CH. The FTIR analysis results also indicated the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which further proving that D-CDs has water solubility. The C1s high resolution of D-CDs showed three parks of 284.7 ev, 285.4 v and 286.1 ev, which corresponding to the C-C/C=C, CO and CO. Besides, the S2p band in Fig. 2G contained three peaks at 163.9 eV for SH,164.6 eV for SS and 165.3 for SC. These three high resolution spectra collectively indicate the successful insertion of S, N atom into the D-CDs. The results showed that DTSA as dopant and one-step hydrothermal synthesis method can achieve effective S atom doping of xylan CDs.

The fluorescence properties of D-CDs and the ability for detection heavy metal Cu2+ were studied. From Fig. 3A, as the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 297 nm to 310 nm, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs at 410 nm gradually decreased, but there was no red and blue shift phenomenon appeared, which indicated that D-CDs has an excitation independent property. Then, fluorescence emission spectroscopy was used to quantitatively analyzed the ability of D-CDs for Cu2+ with excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The results in Fig. 3B showed that when D-CDs solution was cultured with different metal ions (Control, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, F−, Cl−, CN−, HPO4 2−, ClO−, NO2−, SCN−, HS− and Hcy), other metal ions did not have a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs. However, upon the addition of Cu2+ into the D-CDs solution, a notable quenching of the fluorescence signal of D-CDs was observed. Interestingly, other metal ions did not exert a significant influence on the detection of Cu2+. Moreover, we delved deeper into the anti-interference capabilities of D-CDs. When other metal ions were introduced to the D-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity remained relatively unchanged, suggesting that D-CDs exhibited minimal response to these metal ions. Conversely, the addition of Cu2+ resulted in a discernible quenching of the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs, underscoring its excellent anti-interference ability specifically in the detection of Cu2+. These findings collectively demonstrate the high sensitivity and selectivity of D-CDs towards Cu2+. Subsequently, we proposed a mechanism elucidating how D-CDs detect Cu2+. It is postulated that the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surface of D-CDs form electrostatic interactions with Cu2+, facilitating electron transfer. This process leads to the absorption of fluorescence excitation and energy, consequently impeding the fluoresce, as shown in Fig. 3C. Then the effects of different concentrations of Cu2+ on the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs were studied. As depicted in Fig. 3D, with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs at 410 nm gradually quenched, which further proved the selectivity of D-CDs with Cu2+. In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) with Cu2+ concentration ranged from 20 to 140 μM exhibited a good linear relationship with I/I0 = 1.71–0.071[Cu2+], R2 = 0.994. The detection limit of Cu2+ was calculated as 0.64 μM (as shown in Fig. 3E), which was much lower than the maximum limit for Cu2+ in drinking water set by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (1.3 mg/L, ∼20 μM), the standard limit for domestic water applications in China (1.0 mg/L) [43] and the reported Cu2+ fluorescence sensors (Table. 1). In addition, the D-CDs also has good time stability and fluorescence stability when detecting Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 3F. Then the mechanism of Cu2+ detection by D-CDs solution was investigated. The zeta potential analysis results of D-CDs and D-CDs + Cu2+ were − 14.5 mv and − 3.9 mv, respectively, with a large difference in the change of the surface potential of the samples, which further proved that D-CDs chelated with Cu2+, as shown in Fig.S1A. In addition, by fitting the fluorescence decay curves of D-CDs and D-CDs + Cu2+ and calculating the average fluorescence lifetimes of 1.245 ns and 1.224 ns, respectively Fig. S1BC, the values of fluorescence lifetimes did not produce a particularly significant change after the recognition of Cu2+ by D-CDs, which was static bursting.

The fluorescence properties of D-CDs and the ability for detection heavy metal Cu2+ were studied. From Fig. 3A, as the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 297 nm to 310 nm, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs at 410 nm gradually decreased, but there was no red and blue shift phenomenon appeared, which indicated that D-CDs has an excitation independent property. Then, fluorescence emission spectroscopy was used to quantitatively analyzed the ability of D-CDs for Cu2+ with excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The results in Fig. 3B showed that when D-CDs solution was cultured with different metal ions (Control, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, F−, Cl−, CN−, HPO4 2−, ClO−, NO2−, SCN−, HS− and Hcy), other metal ions did not have a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs. However, upon the addition of Cu2+ into the D-CDs solution, a notable quenching of the fluorescence signal of D-CDs was observed. Interestingly, other metal ions did not exert a significant influence on the detection of Cu2+. Moreover, we delved deeper into the anti-interference capabilities of D-CDs. When other metal ions were introduced to the D-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity remained relatively unchanged, suggesting that D-CDs exhibited minimal response to these metal ions. Conversely, the addition of Cu2+ resulted in a discernible quenching of the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs, underscoring its excellent anti-interference ability specifically in the detection of Cu2+. These findings collectively demonstrate the high sensitivity and selectivity of D-CDs towards Cu2+. Subsequently, we proposed a mechanism elucidating how D-CDs detect Cu2+. It is postulated that the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surface of D-CDs form electrostatic interactions with Cu2+, facilitating electron transfer. This process leads to the absorption of fluorescence excitation and energy, consequently impeding the fluoresce, as shown in Fig. 3C. Then the effects of different concentrations of Cu2+ on the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs were studied. As depicted in Fig. 3D, with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs at 410 nm gradually quenched, which further proved the selectivity of D-CDs with Cu2+. In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) with Cu2+ concentration ranged from 20 to 140 μM exhibited a good linear relationship with I/I0 = 1.71–0.071[Cu2+], R2 = 0.994. The detection limit of Cu2+ was calculated as 0.64 μM (as shown in Fig. 3E), which was much lower than the maximum limit for Cu2+ in drinking water set by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (1.3 mg/L, ∼20 μM), the standard limit for domestic water applications in China (1.0 mg/L) [43] and the reported Cu2+ fluorescence sensors (Table. 1). In addition, the D-CDs also has good time stability and fluorescence stability when detecting Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 3F. Then the mechanism of Cu2+ detection by D-CDs solution was investigated. The zeta potential analysis results of D-CDs and D-CDs + Cu2+ were − 14.5 mv and − 3.9 mv, respectively, with a large difference in the change of the surface potential of the samples, which further proved that D-CDs chelated with Cu2+, as shown in Fig.S1A. In addition, by fitting the fluorescence decay curves of D-CDs and D-CDs + Cu2+ and calculating the average fluorescence lifetimes of 1.245 ns and 1.224 ns, respectively Fig. S1BC, the values of fluorescence lifetimes did not produce a particularly significant change after the recognition of Cu2+ by D-CDs, which was static bursting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of NFD-CDs for detection Cu 2+
ce:section.ce:para: In practical scenarios, fluorescent CDs solutions are susceptible to environmental factors like water, heat, and oxygen, which can lead to diminished luminous efficiency or complete quenching. Concurrently, the fluorescence sensing platform based on solid nanofiber membranes has garnered significant interest in environmental monitoring, quality control, and biomedical analysis. This interest stems from its portability, stability, real-time detection capabilities, and ease of manufacturing and integration with flexible devices. Therefore, to enable the detection of Cu2+ in real-world samples such as soil and water, we employ electrospinning technology to merge nanofiber membranes with fluorescent CDs solutions. This fusion results in the creation of a solid-state fluorescence analysis sensor (NFD-CDs) boasting outstanding fluorescence properties, facilitating in-situ, real-time, and on-site detection, as shown in Fig. 4A. The NFD-CDs boasts a considerable specific surface area and porosity, facilitating increased contact with Cu2+ for real-time, in-situ detection. Under daylight conditions, the NFD-CDs appears white color, while under ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), it emits blue fluorescence, confirming the successful integration of D-CDs within the nanofiber membrane, as depicted in Fig. 4B. SEM analysis reveals that the NFD-CDs exhibits a single fiber structure (Fig. 4C) with uniform distribution and an average diameter size of 630 nm (Fig. 4E). EDS analysis further confirms the successful preparation of NFD-CDs (Fig. 4D). Furthermore, we investigated the NFD-CDs' ability to detect Cu2+, as demonstrated in Fig. 4F. Under daylight conditions, the NFD-CDs + Cu2+ combination appeared white, while under ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), it emitted a subdued fluorescence. This decrease in fluorescence intensity compared to Fig. 4B indicates the selective affinity of NFD-CDs for Cu2+. Additionally, NFD-CDs + Cu2+ exhibited a single fiber structure (Fig. 4G) with an average diameter size of 660 nm (Fig. 4H). EDS analysis revealed the presence of three elements—C, O, and Cu2+—confirming the successful preparation of NFD-CDs + Cu2+.

To further investigated the ability of D-CDs for detection Cu2+ in actual soil environments, soil from pond, grassland and bamboo as detection samples were selected. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, D-CDs in soil filter solution showed bright blue fluorescence, whereas, when exogenous Cu2+ (50 μM) was added to above mixed solution, the fluorescence signal of D-CDs showed a significant quenching, which proved that the D-CDs have good potential to detect Cu2+ in the actual environment with quantitative and qualitative analysis.

Reports indicated that Cu2+ concentration poses significant pollution to water bodies, highlighting the importance of detecting Cu2+ pollution [50,51]. To showcase the practical applicability of the D-CDs sensing system, water samples were collected from various sources, including Xuanwu Lake, Yangtze river water, tap water, and bottled water. These samples were then spiked with predetermined concentrations of Cu2+ following a reference method [52], incubated with D-CDs, and their fluorescence intensity was recorded. As depicted in Table 2 , the recovery rate of these samples ranged between 93 % and 102.4 %, demonstrating the effectiveness of D-CDs in detecting Cu2+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility application of D-CDs and NFD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of D-CDs and NFD-CDs were verified, respectively. When the D-CDs solution was co-cultured with Chinese cabbage seeds, the Chinese cabbage seeds showed the same good growth and development as the control group (water), which proved that the D-CDs had no toxic effect on the growth of plants, as shown in Fig. 6A and C. Besides, when NFD-CDs was cultured with adult zebrafish for 14 d, the zebrafish also showed higher survival rate, indicating the high compatibility of NFD-CDs, as shown in Fig. 6B and D.

In essence, the development of a novel biomass-derived fluorescence sensor, D-CDs, for the visual detection of Cu2+ with outstanding stability and sensitivity, has been pioneered by us. With a detection limit of 0.64 μM, this method surpasses the WHO recommendation for Cu2+ levels in drinking water. Furthermore, to enable portable and real-time fluorescence analysis, fluorescence nanofiber membrane (NFD-CDs) has been engineered utilizing electrospinning technology, seamlessly integrating D-CDs with PCL/PLA. This solid-state fluorescence sensor has demonstrated its efficacy in detecting Cu2+ with a colorimetric response, validating its potential as a fiber membrane fluorescence sensor for analyte detection. Additionally, D-CDs have showcased exceptional performance across various water samples, including those sourced from Xuanwu Lake, the Yangtze River, tap water, and bottled water, with successful recovery of spiked Cu2+. These results underscore the potential of this biomass fluorescence sensor in environmental Cu2+ detection, making significant strides in the ongoing efforts to monitor and mitigate Cu2+ environmental pollution.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
(A) Zeta potential change of D-CDs and D-CDs + Cu2+. (B) Fluorescence decay curves of D-CDs. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of D-CDs + Cu2+.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.135037.

Xiuyuan Feng: Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Yingying Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Lihao Zhou: Data curation. Zhiyuan Chen: Methodology, Investigation. Xiaoci Cui: Investigation. Huining Xiao: Resources, Project administration. Anquan Yang: Resources. Minxie: Software, Resources. Ranhua Xiong: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Weixia Cheng: Resources. Chaobo Huang: Resources, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22275094, 22275093) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_1159) for giving us supporting.

Komárek, Stable isotope tracing of Ni and Cu pollution in North-East Norway: Potentials and drawbacks, Environmental Pollution 228 (2017) 149-157.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheet-based turn-on Pseudochemodosimeter as low-cost probe for selective detection of hazardous mercury ion contaminations in water
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Thakuri, A.A. Bhosle, S.D. Hiremath, M. Banerjee, A. Chatterjee, A Carbon Dots-MnO2 Nanosheet-Based Turn-On Pseudochemodosimeter as Low-cost Probe for Selective Detection of Hazardous Mercury Ion Contaminations in Water, Journal of Hazardous Materials. 469 (2024)133998.
